original_hypothesis.title: Bot-Initiated Repay DoS: extraAmount Transfer Pulls from NFT Owner Who Can Block Liquidation by Revoking Approval
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In _repay, when claimedYield < nftDebtWithFine, the contract attempts `underlyingAsset.safeTransferFrom(vars.nftOwner, address(this), vars.extraAmount)`. When botAdmin initiates repay for a liquidation, this pulls funds from the NFT owner's wallet. If the NFT owner has not approved the contract or revokes approval, the transfer reverts, blocking the entire liquidation flow. This is independent of TECH-H001 (which would also block bot repay for a different reason).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The _repay function always pulls extra funds from nftOwner regardless of who calls it. For normal user-initiated repay this is fine, but for bot-initiated liquidation this creates a dependency on the user's approval that the user can weaponize.

proof_of_signal.pattern: underlyingAsset.safeTransferFrom(vars.nftOwner, ...) in bot-callable path without owner's cooperation

preconditions: User has an unhealthy yield position in UNSTAKE state
User revokes WETH approval for the YieldStaking contract

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: safeTransferFrom(vars.nftOwner, ...) when caller may be botAdmin
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: NFT owner can unilaterally block liquidation by not approving or revoking approval
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No alternative path when owner doesn't cooperate

analysis_questions: If the yield claimed doesn't cover debt, is there any way to complete liquidation without owner cooperation?
Can the protocol absorb the shortfall from elsewhere (e.g., unstake fine)?
What is the expected frequency of extraAmount > 0 scenarios?
validation_requirements: Simulate underwater position where claimedYield < debt
Attempt bot repay with owner having revoked approval

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: User stakes NFT and borrows maximum. Market drops, position becomes unhealthy. Bot unstakes the position (setting UNSTAKE state with fine). Before bot can repay, user revokes WETH approval. When bot calls repay and yield doesn't cover debt+fine, safeTransferFrom reverts. Position cannot be settled, debt continues accruing.
prerequisites: Unhealthy yield position where yield < debt
User must have initially approved then revoke

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Lending pool absorbs bad debt from positions that can never be repaid through the bot path
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: NFT owner can voluntarily repay, but has no incentive if underwater

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for bot repay with uncooperative NFT owner

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable as failed bot repay transactions

validation_result.mechanism: Bot-initiated repay DoS via uncooperative NFT owner approval
validation_result.impact_summary: When botAdmin calls _repay and claimed yield < debt+fine, the code attempts safeTransferFrom(nftOwner, ...) which the NFT owner can block by revoking approval, preventing liquidation. Currently also blocked by TECH-H001 (wrong yield account resolution), but this is an independent code defect.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The _repay function in YieldStakingBase.sol unconditionally uses vars.nftOwner as the source for safeTransferFrom when extraAmount > 0 (claimed yield doesn't cover debt + fine). When botAdmin calls repay for a liquidation, this creates an unbreakable dependency on the NFT owner's ERC20 approval to the YieldStaking contract. The NFT owner can trivially block this by revoking their WETH/DAI approval, causing the entire _repay transaction to revert. This is a code defect because the bot-initiated liquidation path should not depend on the debtor's cooperation. The fix would be to pull extraAmount from msg.sender (the bot) or implement an alternative mechanism when the caller is botAdmin.

Note: Currently, this defect is also masked by TECH-H001 (the yield account lookup uses yieldAccounts[msg.sender] instead of sd.yieldAccount, causing bot calls to fail before reaching this line). However, if TECH-H001 were fixed, TECH-H003 would become the active blocking issue for bot-initiated repay.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies the vulnerable pattern. Lines 329-331 of YieldStakingBase.sol show `underlyingAsset.safeTransferFrom(vars.nftOwner, address(this), vars.extraAmount)` in a path that botAdmin can trigger. vars.nftOwner is read from pool token data (the actual NFT owner), not msg.sender. The bot is authorized at line ~314 via `require(vars.nftOwner == msg.sender || botAdmin == msg.sender, ...)` but the fund source is hardcoded to nftOwner.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When botAdmin calls _repay and claimedYield < nftDebtWithFine, the contract attempts safeTransferFrom(vars.nftOwner, ...). The NFT owner can block this by revoking their ERC20 approval to the YieldStaking contract, causing the entire liquidation transaction to revert. The code should pull from msg.sender when the caller is botAdmin, not from nftOwner.

calling_sequence: 1. botAdmin calls repay(poolId, nft, tokenId) or batchRepay(...)
3. vars.yieldAccout = IYieldAccount(yieldAccounts[msg.sender]) -- uses bot's yield account (TECH-H001 issue, but separate)
4. (vars.nftOwner, ...) = poolYield.getERC721TokenData(poolId, nft, tokenId) -- gets actual NFT owner
5. require(vars.nftOwner == msg.sender || botAdmin == msg.sender) -- bot passes check
6. vars.claimedYield = protocolClaimWithdraw(sd) -- claims yield from protocol
7. vars.nftDebtWithFine = vars.nftDebt + sd.unstakeFine -- computes total owed
8. If claimedYield < nftDebtWithFine: vars.extraAmount = nftDebtWithFine - claimedYield
9. underlyingAsset.safeTransferFrom(vars.nftOwner, address(this), vars.extraAmount) -- REVERTS if owner revoked approval
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The safeTransferFrom at step 9 uses vars.nftOwner (the debtor) as the transfer source regardless of who initiated the repay. When botAdmin initiates the repay for liquidation purposes, the debtor (NFT owner) can prevent the transfer by revoking their ERC20 approval to the YieldStaking contract address. This makes the entire bot-initiated liquidation path dependent on the debtor's cooperation, which fundamentally undermines the purpose of having a bot liquidation mechanism.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this defect. The InitConfigYield.s.sol script shows botAdmin is set separately via setBotAdmin(), and the maxUnstakeFine is set to 100 (e.g., for WPUNK). The vulnerability is inherent in the code logic.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario is valid: 1) User stakes NFT and borrows via YieldStaking. 2) Market drops, position becomes unhealthy (HF < unstakeHeathFactor). 3) Bot calls unstake() setting an unstakeFine. 4) User notices and revokes WETH/DAI approval to the YieldStaking contract. 5) When bot calls repay() after withdrawal is ready, if claimedYield < debt + fine (underwater position), safeTransferFrom(nftOwner, ...) reverts due to insufficient allowance. 6) Position cannot be settled through the bot path, debt continues accruing. Note: Currently this is also blocked by TECH-H001 (wrong yield account resolution), but the defect exists independently.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH impact. If TECH-H001 is fixed, this becomes the primary blocker for bot-initiated liquidations of underwater yield positions. Any NFT owner whose position is underwater (yield < debt + fine) has zero incentive to maintain approval and every incentive to revoke it. This leads to: (1) Bad debt accumulation in the lending pool, (2) Inability to clear unhealthy yield positions, (3) Protocol insolvency risk if multiple positions become unliquidatable simultaneously.
recommended_mitigations: When msg.sender is botAdmin, pull extraAmount from msg.sender (the bot) instead of nftOwner, and have the protocol reimburse the bot or add it to the position's debt
Alternatively, implement a mechanism where the protocol absorbs the shortfall from the unstakeFine or from a dedicated insurance fund
Consider allowing partial repayment: repay what's available from claimed yield, and track the remainder as bad debt rather than requiring full payment atomically
Add a fallback path where if extraAmount transfer fails, the position enters a 'bad debt' state that can be resolved through protocol governance
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy full protocol with YieldEthStakingLido (or any strategy). 2. User creates yield account, stakes NFT, borrows WETH. 3. Manipulate price oracle so position becomes unhealthy. 4. Bot calls unstake() with a fine. 5. Wait for withdrawal to be ready. 6. User revokes WETH approval to YieldStaking contract. 7. Bot calls repay() - verify it reverts on safeTransferFrom. Note: Must first fix TECH-H001 (yield account resolution) for the test to reach the vulnerable line.
uncertainties: TECH-H001 currently prevents reaching this code path in practice - the yield account resolution bug would cause revert before the safeTransferFrom line
If both bugs are present simultaneously, TECH-H001 is the primary blocker; TECH-H003 becomes active only after TECH-H001 is fixed
In practice, whether claimedYield < nftDebtWithFine depends on yield protocol performance and accrued interest - healthy positions may always have sufficient yield
additional_testing_needed: Integration test with TECH-H001 fixed to verify TECH-H003 blocks bot repay when owner revokes approval
Stress test with various market conditions to determine frequency of extraAmount > 0 scenarios
Test with YieldSavingsDai which has instant withdrawal (no delay between unstake and repay) to see if user has time to revoke approval
